abstract:
This IUPAC Technical Report provides a comprehensive review and critical evaluation of the various analytical methods used to measure the antioxidant capacity and activity of natural compounds in food, beverages, and biological matrices. Given the chemical complexity of natural antioxidants and their often synergistic effects, determining total antioxidant capacity (TAC) has become a primary objective for food labeling and clinical diagnostics. The report distinguishes between two fundamental reaction mechanisms: hydrogen atom transfer (HAT), such as the ORAC and TRAP assays, and electron transfer (ET), including the FRAP, TEAC, and DPPH methods.

A significant portion of the report focuses on the CUPRAC (cupric reducing antioxidant capacity) method, detailing its versatility in measuring both lipophilic and hydrophilic antioxidants at physiological pH, as well as its various modifications for detecting specific reactive oxygen species and enzyme inhibition. The authors address the technical constraints, kinetic challenges, and lack of comparability between different assays, offering practical recommendations for standardization. Furthermore, the report summarizes the application of these methodologies to a wide range of fruits, vegetables, oils, and cereals, highlighting how processing affects bioactive content. Ultimately, the task group emphasizes that no single assay can fully capture the antioxidant profile of a complex sample, recommending the use of multiple methods with distinct mechanisms to provide a more reliable and multidimensional assessment of antioxidant potential.
